sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on the Rare Fruit Council of Australia and WANATCA archives, Queensland horticultural research, the California Rare Fruit Growers fruit facts, and Australian state agriculture and biosecurity sources.

core.intro: Across Australia the basics of growing a white sapote are the same: a frost-protected spot in full sun, free-draining soil, a grafted named variety, and a little patience while a young, brittle-limbed tree finds its feet. White sapote (Casimiroa edulis) is a citrus relative from the highlands of Mexico and Central America, and one of the more cold-hardy of the subtropical fruits, so it suits a wider range of gardens than most exotic fruit. The state sections cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes below apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: White sapote does not come true from seed: seedlings are highly variable, often slow to bear, and many carry a bitter tang in the skin and around the seeds, so a grafted named variety is worth paying for. Australian nurseries carry a good range of selections made in California, Florida, New Zealand and Australia. Reinekie Commercial, Golden Globe and Lemon Gold were the three that Queensland Department of Primary Industries trials rated best; Suebelle is a popular everbearing variety that crops over a long season and is reliably self-fruitful, which suits a one-tree backyard; Mac's Golden is a golden-skinned selection widely sold here; and Pike, Vista, Vernon, Ortego and Blumenthal are other established cultivars. Decide whether you want a single self-fruitful tree or a pair (see pollination, below), then pick named, grafted stock to match. The in-stock table above shows the named varieties available right now.

sections.heading: Pollination: one tree or two?
sections.body: White sapote flowers are small and perfect, and a single tree will usually set some fruit, so you can start with one. Pollen production, though, varies a lot by cultivar. Some varieties (Ortego and Vernon among them) are reliably self-fruitful, while several popular named cultivars (including Reinekie Commercial, Golden Globe and Blumenthal) carry little viable pollen, so their flowers behave as functionally female and they crop far more heavily with a second, pollen-rich variety nearby. Queensland trials suggested roughly one pollinator tree to every nine of a shy-pollen commercial cultivar. The practical rule for a backyard: if you have room for two trees, plant two different cultivars for heavier, more reliable crops; if you have room for only one, choose a self-fruitful variety such as Suebelle, Ortego or Vernon.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Give white sapote full sun and free-draining soil. It is remarkably adaptable, growing on anything from sand to clay loam and from slightly acid ground to limestone soils near pH 8, though it does best in a free-draining, slightly acid loam of about pH 5.5 to 6.5. The one thing it will not tolerate is wet feet: even a few days of waterlogging can kill a tree, so never plant where water pools, and raise heavy soil into a low mound. Unlike avocado, white sapote is not prone to Phytophthora root rot, which makes it an easier tree on many sites. Young trees have brittle limbs and are easily blown about, so stake them and plant behind a windbreak, and shelter them from frost for their first few winters (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Established white sapotes are drought-hardy, but they need steady moisture to crop well. Water young trees once or twice a week through their first three summers; once a tree is bearing, water deeply only in prolonged dry spells, because it resents overwatering and waterlogged ground. Queensland growers use a deliberate dry spell in late autumn and winter to bring on more even flowering, but avoid stressing the tree for water while it is flowering and setting fruit. For feeding, California and Florida growers use a balanced fertiliser such as a 6:6:6 or 8:3:9, giving a young tree about 100 grams every six to eight weeks and building up to around half a kilogram as it grows, and a mature tree roughly one to two and a half kilograms two or three times a year, plus a few foliar sprays of trace elements across the growing season. Keep the soil weakly acid (about pH 5.5 to 6.5) for the best nutrient uptake.

sections.heading: Training and pruning
sections.body: White sapote has a strong will to grow straight up: left alone, most varieties run a single leader four metres or more with no side branches, then bend over under their own weight. Tip the leader by about a third when the young tree reaches a metre, and tip the new shoots again, and after a few rounds the tree settles into a well-branched, manageable crown. Light, regular pruning over the first three or four years builds fruiting laterals and a leafier canopy that shades the fruit from sunburn. Varieties that set heavy clusters of small fruit are worth thinning to one or two fruit per cluster for better size.

sections.heading: Harvest, ripening and handling
sections.body: White sapote can be picked mature but still hard and ripened indoors with no loss of quality, which is the usual way to handle such a soft, easily bruised fruit. Once it begins to soften, three to five days after picking, it is ready within a day or two and is best eaten when it just yields to gentle pressure, with the flesh soft, sweet and melting. The skin is thin and marks easily, so handle ripe fruit like a ripe peach. Scoop and eat the flesh only: the large seeds, like the leaves and bark, hold sedative compounds and are not edible. Ripe fruit keeps in the fridge for up to about two weeks, and the flesh freezes well. Fruit takes roughly six to nine months to mature from flowering, and because flowering spreads from late autumn into early summer the season is long and varies by district and variety, with everbearing types such as Suebelle carrying fruit over much of the year. A grafted named tree usually starts bearing in about three to five years; a seedling takes seven to ten.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted tree rather than an unnamed seedling: grafted stock fruits years sooner, comes true to type, and avoids the bitterness common in seedlings. Check the label for the cultivar name and whether it is self-fruitful or needs a pollinator, and remember that white sapote is a citrus relative, which matters for getting trees into Western Australia (see the state notes). The in-stock table above shows the named varieties on sale now and what they cost.

faqs.q: What does a white sapote taste like?
faqs.a: Like vanilla custard with hints of peach, pear and banana, and a smooth, creamy texture close to a ripe avocado. Many people describe it as a custard-apple-flavoured avocado, and unlike avocado it is sweet and eaten as a dessert fruit straight from the skin. Named cultivars are reliably sweet, while seedling fruit can carry a slight bitterness.

faqs.q: Is white sapote related to citrus?
faqs.a: Yes. Despite the name and the custard-like fruit, white sapote (Casimiroa edulis) sits in the citrus family, Rutaceae, alongside oranges and lemons; cut a seed in half and it looks like an oversized orange pip. The family link has a practical side: it is why moving trees into Western Australia is restricted (see the WA notes), because the citrus family carries pests and diseases that quarantine works hard to keep out.

faqs.q: Are white sapote seeds safe to eat?
faqs.a: Eat the flesh, not the seeds. The pulp is perfectly safe and delicious, but the large seeds, along with the leaves and bark, contain sedative compounds and are considered toxic, so do not eat them; the old Aztec name for the fruit translates as the sleepy sapote. Just scoop the flesh and discard the one to five orange-pip-like seeds, as you would with a stone fruit.

faqs.q: How is white sapote different from black sapote?
faqs.a: They are unrelated trees that happen to share the word sapote. White sapote (Casimiroa edulis) is a citrus relative with creamy, custard-like flesh and a vanilla flavour, and it is fairly cold-hardy. Black sapote (Diospyros nigra) is a persimmon relative whose dark flesh has earned it the name chocolate pudding fruit, and it needs a warmer, frost-free, tropical to subtropical climate.

QLD.intro: Growing white sapote in Queensland. White sapote is a highland subtropical fruit that dislikes humidity, so in Queensland it is a tree for the cooler, drier south-east and the elevated inland, not the wet tropical coast. Queensland did the foundational Australian research on it, trialling dozens of cultivars.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: White sapote comes from dry highland forests and, in the words of one long-time grower, hates lowland humid tropical conditions. In Queensland that means it crops best in the drier, cooler south-east and on elevated inland sites, while along the wet far-northern coast the tree grows happily but sets and ripens fruit poorly. In the south-east it flowers through the coolest winter months, around July and August.

sections.heading: Where it grows in Queensland
sections.body: The Queensland Department of Primary Industries assessed about forty cultivars at the Maroochy Horticultural Research Station near Nambour, on the Sunshine Coast, and trialled fruit around Rockhampton in central Queensland. Backyard and small-orchard trees do well through the Sunshine Coast hinterland and the Gympie district, where the drier, more elevated country suits the tree far better than the humid coastal strip.

sections.heading: Harvest window in Queensland
sections.body: In the south-east, white sapote matures from about early November to late January. Warmer districts can ripen up to a month earlier, while cooler, more elevated subtropical areas run two to three months later, and everbearing types stretch the season further still.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in Queensland
sections.body: The main pests are Queensland fruit fly, which will sting the fruit even while it is still hard, plus the fruit spotting bug, soft brown scale and mealybugs. White sapote's resistance to Phytophthora root rot is a genuine advantage in the wetter east. Net or bait for fruit fly as the fruit nears maturity.

faqs.q: Which variety should I plant in the south-east?
faqs.a: The cultivars that performed best in the Queensland trials were Reinekie Commercial (large, strong flavour, easy to judge for ripeness), Golden Globe (small, round, excellent flavour) and Lemon Gold (mild, with good keeping). For the heaviest crops, pair a shy-pollen commercial cultivar with a pollen-rich one nearby.

faqs.q: Is it grown commercially in this state?
faqs.a: Only on a small scale. The soft fruit must be eaten within a few days of softening, and that short shelf life, rather than any lack of yield, has kept it a backyard and farmers-market tree rather than a supermarket crop, despite heavy local yields and the research effort behind it.

WA.intro: Growing white sapote in Western Australia. The Mediterranean south-west, with its hot dry summers and mild winters, suits white sapote about as well as anywhere in the country, and the tree is hardy enough to take the region's light frosts. The catch is getting one: white sapote is a citrus relative, so WA's strict citrus-family import rules limit which interstate nurseries can send it here.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: White sapote thrives in a warm, dry climate, and the south-west's Mediterranean summers and mild, wet winters suit it well; growers have long rated it a must from Perth across to Adelaide. As one of the more cold-hardy subtropical fruits it shrugs off the light frosts of the south-west once established, and the dry summer air brings far less fruit fly and fungal pressure than the humid eastern states. In the hot Gascoyne and the north it crops well with irrigation.

sections.heading: Where it grows in WA
sections.body: White sapote does well in gardens across the Swan Coastal Plain, through the cooler Great Southern with a little frost shelter for young trees, and under irrigation in the warm Gascoyne around Carnarvon. Its tolerance of alkaline, free-draining ground means it copes with the limestone-influenced sands that defeat fussier fruit trees.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: White sapote belongs to the citrus family (Rutaceae), and Western Australia has strict rules on importing citrus and its relatives to keep out serious citrus pests and diseases. In practice that means most eastern-states nurseries will not post white sapote trees to WA, so the in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship here, and a locally grown tree is often the easiest option.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: Western Australia has Mediterranean fruit fly rather than Queensland fruit fly, and it will sting ripening white sapote, so net or bait the tree as the fruit colours up. Soft brown scale and mealybugs are minor and easily managed, and the dry climate keeps fungal problems low.

faqs.q: Will it grow in Perth's sandy, alkaline soils?
faqs.a: Yes. White sapote is one of the more soil-tolerant fruit trees, handling everything from sand to clay and from slightly acid ground up to alkaline, limestone-influenced soils, as long as drainage is sharp. Improve sandy ground with compost and mulch, use a wetting agent if the sand repels water, and feed little and often because nutrients leach quickly.

faqs.q: Does it need much water through the dry summer?
faqs.a: Young trees do: water them once or twice a week through their first few summers to get them established. A mature tree is drought-hardy and only needs a deep soak during long dry spells, though keeping the water up while the fruit is filling gives a better crop. Avoid waterlogging at any age.

NSW.intro: Growing white sapote in New South Wales. White sapote has one of the longest backyard histories in the state, fruiting in the Sydney basin for generations, and its unusual cold-hardiness lets it grow well beyond the warm coast.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: White sapote has been grown and fruited around Sydney for decades, and because it is one of the more cold-hardy subtropical fruits, taking light frost once established, it suits a wide sweep of New South Wales, from the warm coast to the cooler, frost-prone inland and the edges of the tablelands with a sheltered, north-facing spot. It prefers a drier, sunnier position to the most humid coastal pockets.

sections.heading: Where it grows in NSW
sections.body: It is an old favourite in the Sydney basin and on the Central Coast, grows through the subtropical Northern Rivers hinterland (the district around Lismore where rare-fruit nurseries first trialled many cultivars), and into the warm inland valleys. The cooler tablelands need a warm, sheltered position and frost protection for young trees.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: Fruit ripens through summer and into autumn on the coast, and later in the cooler districts; everbearing types such as Suebelle carry fruit over a longer spread of the year, which is part of the appeal of a backyard tree.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is the main pest in New South Wales and will sting the fruit while it is still hard, so net or bait as the crop nears maturity. The fruit spotting bug can damage fruit in the warmer north, and soft brown scale is an occasional, easily managed nuisance.

faqs.q: How big does the tree get, and can I keep it smaller in a suburban garden?
faqs.a: A seedling can reach fifteen metres or more, but a grafted named tree is smaller, around ten metres, and responds well to pruning. Tip the leader and shorten the laterals while it is young and you can hold it to three or four metres, an easy size to net and pick in a Sydney backyard.

faqs.q: Can I grow one from a seed out of a shop-bought fruit?
faqs.a: You can, and fresh seed germinates readily, but the seedling will be a gamble: variable in quality, often slightly bitter, and seven to ten years to its first fruit. For known quality and fruit in three to five years, graft it onto your seedling or simply buy a grafted, named tree.

VIC.intro: Growing white sapote in Victoria. Victoria is the cool edge of white sapote's range, but because it is hardier than most subtropical fruit it can be grown around Melbourne and in the milder districts with a warm, sheltered spot.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: White sapote is one of the hardier subtropical fruits, with mature trees taking light frosts down to about minus three degrees Celsius, so it can be grown around Melbourne and in the warmer, frost-protected parts of Victoria, though it ripens its fruit later than the northern states. Young trees are frost-tender and must be protected through their first winters, and the coldest inland frost hollows are too cold for reliable cropping.

sections.heading: Where it grows in VIC
sections.body: It suits warm, sheltered, north-facing gardens around Melbourne and the milder coastal districts such as the Mornington Peninsula and parts of Gippsland. A large pot that can be wheeled out of the hardest frost is a practical way to grow it this far south.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Victoria ripens white sapote latest of the mainland states, mainly through autumn, because the cooler season slows the fruit by two to three months compared with the subtropical north.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is established in parts of Victoria, so net or bait where it occurs, but cool conditions keep overall pest and disease pressure low, and the Phytophthora root rot that troubles avocados is not a problem for white sapote.

faqs.q: Is a potted white sapote a good idea in Melbourne?
faqs.a: It is one of the better ways to grow it here. A large container with a free-draining mix lets you stand the tree in the sunniest, most sheltered corner and move it under cover during the worst frosts and cold snaps, then back out for summer. Water and feed regularly, since pots dry out and leach nutrients fast.

faqs.q: Which variety suits a cool Melbourne garden?
faqs.a: A self-fruitful, everbearing type such as Suebelle is the safe single-tree choice, because it sets fruit without a pollinator and crops over a long, mild stretch rather than all at once. Give it the warmest, most sheltered position you have, and protect it from frost while it is young.
